content:
# Genesis Chapter 36 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 20v–21v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_036_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_036_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_036_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 33–35 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0037 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0038 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0039

---

*The Toledot of Esau — His Wives and Sons in Canaan (vv. 1–5)*

**36:1** And these are the generations [וְאֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת] of Esau [עֵשָׂו] — he is Edom [הוּא אֱדוֹם].

**36:2** Esau took [לָקַח עֵשָׂו] his wives [אֶת-נָשָׁיו] from the daughters of Canaan [מִבְּנוֹת כְּנָעַן] — Adah [עָדָה] the daughter of Elon [בַּת-אֵילוֹן] the Hittite [הַחִתִּי] — and Oholibamah [וְאָהֳלִיבָמָה] the daughter of Anah [בַּת-עֲנָה] the daughter of Zibeon [בַּת-צִבְעוֹן] the Hivite [הַחִוִּי] —

**36:3** and Basemath [וּבָשְׂמַת] the daughter of Ishmael [בַּת-יִשְׁמָעֵאל] — the sister of Nebaioth [אֲחוֹת נְבָיוֹת].

**36:4** And Adah bore [וַתֵּלֶד עָדָה] to Esau [לְעֵשָׂו] — Eliphaz [אֶת-אֱלִיפָז] — and Basemath bore [וּבָשְׂמַת יָלְדָה] — Reuel [אֶת-רְעוּאֵל].

**36:5** And Oholibamah bore [וְאָהֳלִיבָמָה יָלְדָה] — Jeush [אֶת-יְעוּשׁ] and Jalam [וְאֶת-יַעְלָם] and Korah [וְאֶת-קֹרַח] — these are the sons of Esau [אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי עֵשָׂו] who were born to him [אֲשֶׁר יֻלְּדוּ-לוֹ] in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן].

---

*Esau's Migration to Seir — The Separation from Jacob (vv. 6–8)*

**36:6** And Esau took [וַיִּקַּח עֵשָׂו] his wives [אֶת-נָשָׁיו] and his sons [וְאֶת-בָּנָיו] and his daughters [וְאֶת-בְּנֹתָיו] and all the persons of his household [וְאֶת-כָּל-נַפְשׁוֹת בֵּיתוֹ] and his livestock [וְאֶת-מִקְנֵהוּ] and all his beasts [וְאֶת-כָּל-בְּהֶמְתּוֹ] and all his possessions [וְאֶת-כָּל-קִנְיָנוֹ] that he had acquired [אֲשֶׁר רָכַשׁ] in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] — and he went [וַיֵּלֶךְ] to a land [אֶל-אֶרֶץ] away from the face of [מִפְּנֵי] Jacob his brother [יַעֲקֹב אָחִיו].

**36:7** For their possessions [כִּי-הָיָה רְכוּשָׁם] were too great [רָב] for them [מֵהֶם] to dwell together [מִשֶּׁבֶת יַחְדָּו] — and the land [וְלֹא יָכְלָה הָאָרֶץ] of their sojournings [מְגוּרֵיהֶם] was not able [לֹא יָכְלָה] to sustain them [לָשֵׂאת אֹתָם] because of their livestock [מִפְּנֵי מִקְנֵיהֶם].

**36:8** And Esau dwelt [וַיֵּשֶׁב עֵשָׂו] in the hill country of Seir [בְּהַר שֵׂעִיר] — Esau [עֵשָׂו] — he is Edom [הוּא אֱדוֹם].

---

*The Toledot of Esau in Seir — Sons and Grandsons (vv. 9–14)*

**36:9** And these are the generations [וְאֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת] of Esau [עֵשָׂו] — the father of Edom [אֲבִי אֱדוֹם] — in the hill country of Seir [בְּהַר שֵׂעִיר].

**36:10** These are the names [אֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת] of the sons of Esau [בְּנֵי-עֵשָׂו] — Eliphaz [אֱלִיפַז] — the son of Adah [בֶּן-עָדָה] the wife of Esau [אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו] — Reuel [רְעוּאֵל] — the son of Basemath [בֶּן-בָּשְׂמַת] the wife of Esau [אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו].

**36:11** And the sons of Eliphaz were [וַיִּהְיוּ בְּנֵי אֱלִיפַז] — Teman [תֵּימָן] — Omar [אוֹמָר] — Zepho [צְפוֹ] — and Gatam [וְגַעְתָּם] — and Kenaz [וּקְנַז].

**36:12** And Timna [וְתִמְנַע] was a concubine [הָיְתָה פִילֶגֶשׁ] of Eliphaz [לֶאֱלִיפַז] the son of Esau [בֶּן-עֵשָׂו] — and she bore [וַתֵּלֶד] to Eliphaz [לֶאֱלִיפַז] — Amalek [אֶת-עֲמָלֵק] — these are the sons of Adah [אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי עָדָה] the wife of Esau [אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו].

**36:13** And these are the sons of Reuel [וְאֵלֶּה בְּנֵי רְעוּאֵל] — Nahath [נַחַת] and Zerah [וָזֶרַח] — Shammah [שַׁמָּה] and Mizzah [וּמִזָּה] — these were the sons of Basemath [אֵלֶּה הָיוּ בְּנֵי בָשְׂמַת] the wife of Esau [אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו].

**36:14** And these were the sons of Oholibamah [וְאֵלֶּה הָיוּ בְנֵי אָהֳלִיבָמָה] the daughter of Anah [בַּת-עֲנָה] — daughter of Zibeon [בַּת-צִבְעוֹן] — the wife of Esau [אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו] — and she bore to Esau [וַתֵּלֶד לְעֵשָׂו] — Jeush [אֶת-יְעוּשׁ] and Jalam [וְאֶת-יַעְלָם] and Korah [וְאֶת-קֹרַח].

---

*The Chiefs of the Sons of Esau (vv. 15–19)*

**36:15** These are the chiefs [אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי] of the sons of Esau [בְּנֵי-עֵשָׂו] — the sons of Eliphaz [בְּנֵי אֱלִיפַז] the firstborn of Esau [בְּכוֹר עֵשָׂו] — chief Teman [אַלּוּף תֵּימָן] — chief Omar [אַלּוּף אוֹמָר] — chief Zepho [אַלּוּף צְפוֹ] — chief Kenaz [אַלּוּף קְנַז] —

**36:16** chief Korah [אַלּוּף קֹרַח] — chief Gatam [אַלּוּף גַּעְתָּם] — chief Amalek [אַלּוּף עֲמָלֵק] — these are the chiefs [אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי] of Eliphaz [אֱלִיפַז] in the land of Edom [בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם] — these are the sons of Adah [אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי עָדָה].

**36:17** And these are the sons of Reuel [וְאֵלֶּה בְּנֵי רְעוּאֵל] — the son of Esau [בֶּן-עֵשָׂו] — chief Nahath [אַלּוּף נַחַת] — chief Zerah [אַלּוּף זֶרַח] — chief Shammah [אַלּוּף שַׁמָּה] — chief Mizzah [אַלּוּף מִזָּה] — these are the chiefs of Reuel [אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי רְעוּאֵל] in the land of Edom [בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם] — these are the sons of Basemath [אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי בָשְׂמַת] the wife of Esau [אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו].

**36:18** And these are the sons of Oholibamah [וְאֵלֶּה בְנֵי אָהֳלִיבָמָה] the wife of Esau [אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו] — chief Jeush [אַלּוּף יְעוּשׁ] — chief Jalam [אַלּוּף יַעְלָם] — chief Korah [אַלּוּף קֹרַח] — these are the chiefs of Oholibamah [אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי אָהֳלִיבָמָה] the daughter of Anah [בַּת-עֲנָה] the wife of Esau [אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו].

**36:19** These are the sons of Esau [אֵלֶּה בְנֵי-עֵשָׂו] and these are their chiefs [וְאֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵיהֶם] — he is Edom [הוּא אֱדוֹם].

---

*The Sons of Seir the Horite — The Pre-Edomite Inhabitants (vv. 20–30)*

**36:20** These are the sons of Seir [אֵלֶּה בְנֵי-שֵׂעִיר] the Horite [הַחֹרִי] — the inhabitants of the land [יֹשְׁבֵי הָאָרֶץ] — Lotan [לוֹטָן] and Shobal [וְשׁוֹבָל] and Zibeon [וְצִבְעוֹן] and Anah [וַעֲנָה] —

**36:21** and Dishon [וְדִשׁוֹן] and Ezer [וְאֵצֶר] and Dishan [וְדִישָׁן] — these are the chiefs [אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי] of the Horites [הַחֹרִי] — the sons of Seir [בְנֵי שֵׂעִיר] — in the land of Edom [בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם].

**36:22** And the sons of Lotan were [וַיִּהְיוּ בְנֵי-לוֹטָן] — Hori [חֹרִי] and Hemam [וְהֵימָם] — and the sister of Lotan [וַאֲחוֹת לוֹטָן] was Timna [תִּמְנָע].

**36:23** And these are the sons of Shobal [וְאֵלֶּה בְּנֵי שׁוֹבָל] — Alvan [עַלְוָן] and Manahath [וּמָנַחַת] and Ebal [וְעֵיבָל] — Shepho [שְׁפוֹ] and Onam [וְאוֹנָם].

**36:24** And these are the sons of Zibeon [וְאֵלֶּה בְנֵי-צִבְעוֹן] — Aiah [אַיָּה] and Anah [וַעֲנָה] — he is the Anah [הוּא עֲנָה] who found the hot springs [אֲשֶׁר מָצָא אֶת-הַיֵּמִם] in the wilderness [בַּמִּדְבָּר] when he tended the donkeys [בִּרְעֹתוֹ אֶת-הַחֲמֹרִים] of Zibeon [לְצִבְעוֹן] his father [אָבִיו].

**36:25** And these are the children of Anah [וְאֵלֶּה בְּנֵי עֲנָה] — Dishon [דִּשׁוֹן] and Oholibamah [וְאָהֳלִיבָמָה] the daughter of Anah [בַּת-עֲנָה].

**36:26** And these are the sons of Dishon [וְאֵלֶּה בְּנֵי דִישׁוֹן] — Hemdan [חֶמְדָּן] and Eshban [וְאֶשְׁבָּן] and Ithran [וְיִתְרָן] and Cheran [וּכְרָן].

**36:27** These are the sons of Ezer [אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי-אֵצֶר] — Bilhan [בִּלְהָן] and Zaavan [וְזַעֲוָן] and Akan [וַעֲקָן].

**36:28** These are the sons of Dishan [אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי-דִישָׁן] — Uz [עוּץ] and Aran [וַאֲרָן].

**36:29** These are the chiefs of the Horites [אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי הַחֹרִי] — chief Lotan [אַלּוּף לוֹטָן] — chief Shobal [אַלּוּף שׁוֹבָל] — chief Zibeon [אַלּוּף צִבְעוֹן] — chief Anah [אַלּוּף עֲנָה] —

**36:30** chief Dishon [אַלּוּף דִּשׁוֹן] — chief Ezer [אַלּוּף אֵצֶר] — chief Dishan [אַלּוּף דִּישָׁן] — these are the chiefs of the Horites [אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי הַחֹרִי] by their chiefs [לְאַלֻּפֵיהֶם] in the land of Seir [בְּאֶרֶץ שֵׂעִיר].

---

*The Kings of Edom Who Reigned Before Israel Had a King (vv. 31–39)*

**36:31** And these are the kings [וְאֵלֶּה הַמְּלָכִים] who reigned [אֲשֶׁר מָלְכוּ] in the land of Edom [בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם] before any king reigned [לִפְנֵי מְלָךְ-מֶלֶךְ] over the sons of Israel [לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל].

**36:32** And Bela the son of Beor [וַיִּמְלֹךְ בְּאֱדוֹם בֶּלַע בֶּן-בְּעוֹר] reigned in Edom — and the name of his city [וְשֵׁם עִירוֹ] was Dinhabah [דִּנְהָבָה].

**36:33** And Bela died [וַיָּמָת בֶּלַע] — and Jobab the son of Zerah [וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו יוֹבָב בֶּן-זֶרַח] from Bozrah [מִבָּצְרָה] reigned in his place.

**36:34** And Jobab died [וַיָּמָת יוֹבָב] — and Husham [וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו חֻשָׁם] from the land of the Temanites [מֵאֶרֶץ הַתֵּימָנִי] reigned in his place.

**36:35** And Husham died [וַיָּמָת חֻשָׁם] — and Hadad the son of Bedad [וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו הֲדַד בֶּן-בְּדַד] — who struck Midian [הַמַּכֶּה אֶת-מִדְיָן] in the field of Moab [בִּשְׂדֵה מוֹאָב] — reigned in his place — and the name of his city [וְשֵׁם עִירוֹ] was Avith [עֲוִית].

**36:36** And Hadad died [וַיָּמָת הֲדַד] — and Samlah [וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו שַׂמְלָה] from Masrekah [מִמַּשְׂרֵקָה] reigned in his place.

**36:37** And Samlah died [וַיָּמָת שַׂמְלָה] — and Shaul [וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו שָׁאוּל] from Rehoboth-by-the-river [מֵרְחֹבוֹת הַנָּהָר] reigned in his place.

**36:38** And Shaul died [וַיָּמָת שָׁאוּל] — and Baal-hanan the son of Achbor [וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו בַּעַל חָנָן בֶּן-עַכְבּוֹר] reigned in his place.

**36:39** And Baal-hanan the son of Achbor died [וַיָּמָת בַּעַל חָנָן בֶּן-עַכְבּוֹר] — and Hadar [וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו הֲדַר] reigned in his place — and the name of his city [וְשֵׁם עִירוֹ] was Pau [פָּעוּ] — and his wife's name [וְשֵׁם אִשְׁתּוֹ] was Mehetabel [מְהֵיטַבְאֵל] the daughter of Matred [בַּת-מַטְרֵד] the daughter of Me-zahab [בַּת מֵי זָהָב].

---

*The Chiefs of Esau by Clans and Territories (vv. 40–43)*

**36:40** And these are the names [וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת] of the chiefs of Esau [אַלּוּפֵי עֵשָׂו] by their families [לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם] by their places [לִמְקֹמֹתָם] by their names [בִּשְׁמֹתָם] — chief Timna [אַלּוּף תִּמְנָע] — chief Alvah [אַלּוּף עַלְוָה] — chief Jetheth [אַלּוּף יְתֵת] —

**36:41** chief Oholibamah [אַלּוּף אָהֳלִיבָמָה] — chief Elah [אַלּוּף אֵלָה] — chief Pinon [אַלּוּף פִּינֹן] —

**36:42** chief Kenaz [אַלּוּף קְנַז] — chief Teman [אַלּוּף תֵּימָן] — chief Mibzar [אַלּוּף מִבְצָר] —

**36:43** chief Magdiel [אַלּוּף מַגְדִּיאֵל] — chief Iram [אַלּוּף עִירָם] — these are the chiefs of Edom [אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי אֱדוֹם] by their settlements [לְמֹשְׁבֹתָם] in the land of their possession [בְּאֶרֶץ אֲחֻזָּתָם] — he is Esau [הוּא עֵשָׂו] — the father of Edom [אֲבִי אֱדוֹם].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 20v–21v:**

Folio 20v (GEN_036_page_001.jpg) is the verso of the twentieth leaf of the Leningrad Codex Genesis section, photographed in color by the West Semitic Research Project. The folio number כ (twenty) and the corresponding verso designation are visible in the upper margin. The three-column Tiberian-vocalized Sephardic square script layout is clearly legible throughout, though the right-hand edge of the rightmost column carries slight wear from the page turn. The folio opens with the final verses of Genesis 35 in the right column — Isaac's death and the burial notice — and Genesis 36 begins in the middle or left column, marked by a clear **parasha** (section) break. The opening formula **וְאֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת עֵשָׂו** ("and these are the generations of Esau") introduces the chapter with the standard *toledot* heading used throughout Genesis. Full Tiberian cantillation marks (*te'amim*) and vowel points (*niqud*) are present and legible throughout. The genealogical lists are formatted as running prose, not as columnar lists; the repetitive formulaic structure (chief X, chief Y) produces a rhythmic visual pattern on the page that differs markedly from the narrative text of surrounding chapters. Masorah marginalia are visible in the outer margin, with individual Masoretic notes (*masorah parva*) alongside specific verses; the *masorah magna* at the lower margin records word-count data. No lacunae or damage is present in the area of Genesis 36 on this folio.

Folio 21r (GEN_036_page_002.jpg) carries the bulk of Genesis 36 — from approximately verse 15 through verse 36 — in its three columns. The folio marker כ״א (twenty-one) is visible at the upper margin. The three-column layout continues with full vocalization and cantillation. The Horite genealogy (vv. 20–30) and the Edomite king-list (vv. 31–39) are fully legible. The king-list section is visually striking: the repeated formula **וַיָּמָת ... וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו** ("and X died, and Y reigned in his place") runs through six successive verses (vv. 32–37) creating a rhythmic cadence on the folio. The scribe's hand is consistent throughout. A *setumah* (closed section break) space is visible in the line before verse 31, signaling the shift from the Horite genealogy to the king-list. Similarly, a *setumah* space appears before verse 40, marking the transition to the final chiefs-list. The right margin of the folio is slightly tighter, with some letters compressed toward the edge, but all text remains fully legible.

Folio 21v (GEN_036_page_003.jpg) carries the conclusion of Genesis 36 (vv. 37–43) in its opening columns, followed by the beginning of Genesis 37 (the Joseph narrative). The three-column layout is intact and fully legible. The conclusion of Genesis 36 at verse 43 — **הוּא עֵשָׂו אֲבִי אֱדוֹם** ("he is Esau, the father of Edom") — provides a closing identification formula that mirrors the opening of verse 1. The *parashah* break that separates Genesis 36 from Genesis 37 is clearly visible as a larger *petuhah* (open section break), with blank space in the line and a new line beginning, signaling the major narrative transition from the Edomite genealogy to the Jacob-Joseph story. The masorah in the lower margin records the *seder* division marker relevant to the synagogue reading cycle.

---

**36:1 — *toledot* (תֹּלְדוֹת) — "Generations": The Toledot Formula:**

The **תֹּלְדוֹת** (*toledot*, "generations, genealogy, offspring-record") formula — **וְאֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת** ("and these are the generations of") — is the structural spine of the book of Genesis, appearing eleven times and dividing the book into its major sections (Genesis 2:4; 5:1; 6:9; 10:1; 11:10; 11:27; 25:12; 25:19; 36:1; 36:9; 37:2). Genesis 36 is unique in containing *two* toledot headings: the formula appears in verse 1 ("the generations of Esau, he is Edom") and again in verse 9 ("the generations of Esau, father of Edom, in the hill country of Seir"), an unusual structural doublet. The first *toledot* (v.1) introduces Esau's family formed while still in Canaan; the second (v.9) introduces his descendants as established in the hill country of Seir — as though the material is organized as two originally separate genealogical traditions now joined. The word *toledot* itself is from the root יָלַד (*yalad*, "to beget, to give birth"), the same root used for birth notices throughout: it is, literally, "the begettings-forth" of Esau — what Esau produced, genealogically and historically.

The identification formula **הוּא אֱדוֹם** ("he is Edom") appears three times in the chapter: v.1, v.8, and v.19. This triple identification of Esau with Edom is emphatic — the narrator is establishing a theological and political equation that will persist through the entire Hebrew Bible: Esau *is* Edom. The nation is the man, and the man is the nation.

---

**36:2–3 — Esau's Wives: The Name Discrepancy with Genesis 26:34 and 28:9:**

This is one of the most thoroughly discussed textual difficulties in Genesis. The discrepancy between the wife-names in Genesis 36 and their earlier attestations is substantial:

| Here (Gen 36) | Earlier Reference | Name in Earlier Text |
|---|---|---|
| Adah (עָדָה), daughter of Elon the Hittite | Gen 26:34 | Judith (יְהוּדִית), daughter of Beeri the Hittite; Basemath (בָּשְׂמַת), daughter of Elon the Hittite |
| Oholibamah (אָהֳלִיבָמָה), daughter of Anah daughter of Zibeon the Hivite | Gen 26:34 | (No Oholibamah in Gen 26) |
| Basemath (בָּשְׂמַת), daughter of Ishmael | Gen 28:9 | Mahalath (מָחֲלַת), daughter of Ishmael |

The harmonization problem is fourfold: (1) the name Adah here does not match Judith or Basemath from Genesis 26; (2) Basemath in Genesis 26:34 is identified as daughter of Elon the Hittite, while in Genesis 36 *Adah* is daughter of Elon and *Basemath* is daughter of Ishmael; (3) Oholibamah, prominent here as the third wife, does not appear in Genesis 26–28 at all; (4) the Ishmael-daughter is called Basemath here but Mahalath in Genesis 28:9. No entirely satisfactory harmonization exists. The dominant critical reading is that the wife-names in Genesis 36 come from a different source tradition (often associated with the P source) than the Genesis 26–28 narratives (associated with J or E), and that the canonical text has preserved both traditions without smoothing the discrepancy. The ancient translators and harmonizers (LXX, Jubilees, Josephus) attempt various reconciliations, typically assigning the different names as alternate names for the same women.

EMET translates the names as they stand in Genesis 36 without harmonizing to Genesis 26–28. The Leningrad Codex text is clear and unambiguous on all three wife names in this chapter.

---

**36:6–7 — *mippenei Ya'aqov achiv* (מִפְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב אָחִיו) and the Abraham-Lot Echo:**

The formula **כִּי-הָיָה רְכוּשָׁם רָב מִשֶּׁבֶת יַחְדָּו** ("for their possessions were too great for them to dwell together") echoes Genesis 13:6 precisely: **וְלֹא-נָשָׂא אֹתָם הָאָרֶץ לָשֶׁבֶת יַחְדָּו כִּי-הָיָה רְכוּשָׁם רָב** ("the land could not sustain them to dwell together, for their possessions were great"). The verbal parallel is exact — the same root רָב (*rav*, "great, numerous"), the same phrase *mishevet yachdav* ("from dwelling together"), the same problem of livestock-wealth exceeding the land's carrying capacity. As Abraham and Lot separated at that earlier moment (Lot going toward the Jordan/Sodom; Abraham remaining in Canaan), so now Esau separates from Jacob (moving to Seir; Jacob remaining in Canaan). The structural parallel is intentional: the Esau-Jacob separation is the third-generation recapitulation of the Abraham-Lot separation. In both cases the separation is peaceful — not a rupture but a natural division of territory necessitated by prosperity.

The phrase **מִפְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב אָחִיו** ("from the face of Jacob his brother") uses the idiom *mippenei* ("from the face of, away from the presence of") — the same idiom used for Cain fleeing "from the face of the LORD" (Gen 4:16) and for Jacob himself fleeing "from the face of Esau" (Gen 35:1). There is no hostility implied here in Genesis 36's usage — the departure is economic, not fearful.

---

**36:8 — *vayeshev Esav behar Se'ir* (וַיֵּשֶׁב עֵשָׂו בְּהַר שֵׂעִיר) — Esau in Seir: Geography and the Name:**

**הַר שֵׂעִיר** (*Har Se'ir*, "the hill country of Seir" or "Mount Seir") is the highland region south and southeast of the Dead Sea, in the area of modern southern Jordan and the Arabah. The name שֵׂעִיר (*Se'ir*) means "hairy" or "rough/shaggy" — the same root as עֵשָׂו (*Esav*, related to the tradition that Esau was hairy at birth, Gen 25:25). The identification of Esau/Edom with the hairy land of Seir is thus an onomastic pun embedded in the geography: the hairy man occupies the hairy mountain. The region was indeed the heartland of Edom historically, confirmed by archaeological survey and Egyptian topographical texts from the New Kingdom period. The triple identification formula **עֵשָׂו הוּא אֱדוֹם** ("Esau, he is Edom") that closes verse 8 underlines the equation once more.

---

**36:12 — *Timna hayetah filegesh le-Eliphaz* (וְתִמְנַע הָיְתָה פִילֶגֶשׁ לֶאֱלִיפַז) — Timna, Amalek's Mother: The Origin of Israel's Arch-Enemy:**

The genealogical placement of Amalek's birth is carefully crafted. Timna (תִּמְנַע) is not a wife (*ishah*) but a **פִּילֶגֶשׁ** (*pilegesh*, "concubine") — a secondary wife with legal but lower status than the primary wife. Amalek is thus the son of Eliphaz (Esau's firstborn) but through a concubine. His status is legitimate but secondary — he is a grandson of Esau, in the Edomite line, but born of a Horite woman (Timna is identified in v.22 as the sister of Lotan the Horite). This genealogical positioning makes Amalek a figure who straddles the Edomite and Horite worlds, born of mixed Esau-Seir descent.

The importance of this verse cannot be overstated within the larger biblical narrative. **עֲמָלֵק** (*Amalek*) becomes, in later texts, Israel's paradigmatic enemy — the nation that attacked Israel from behind in the desert (Exodus 17:8–16), whose destruction is commanded absolutely by Samuel (1 Samuel 15), and whose last scion (Haman the Agagite) attempts genocide against Israel in Esther. Every subsequent mention of Amalek in the Hebrew Bible traces back to this single genealogical verse: Timna's son by Eliphaz, grandson of Esau. The biblical writers embedded the origin of Israel's most persistent enemy within the genealogy of Esau's own firstborn son — a pointed theological commentary on the danger residing within kinship.

Timna appears again as the sister of Lotan (v.22) — a Horite. This confirms her Horite origin, meaning the concubine of Eliphaz was herself from the pre-Edomite indigenous population of Seir. Amalek is thus the product of Edomite-Horite intermingling.

---

**36:15–19 — *allufim* (אַלּוּפִים) — "Chiefs": Edomite Political Terminology:**

The term **אַלּוּף** (*aluf*, plural **אַלּוּפִים** *allufim*) — translated here consistently as "chief" per project convention — is a term of particular interest. Its etymology is debated. The word relates to the root אָלַף (*alaf*), which in one sense means "to learn, to be familiar with" and in another yields **אֶלֶף** (*elef*, "thousand") — leading some scholars to posit that an *aluf* was originally a "commander of a thousand" or a "clan-leader." However, in the context of Genesis 36, the term functions as a political/tribal title specific to Edom and the Horites: it appears 14 times in this chapter alone and is otherwise relatively rare in the Hebrew Bible. EMET renders it "chief" throughout rather than "duke" (KJV) or "clan chief" to maintain clarity and consistency with project convention.

The structural function of the *allufim* lists is significant: the chapter provides three overlapping lists of chiefs — (1) the chiefs of Esau's sons (vv. 15–19), (2) the chiefs of the Horites (vv. 20–30, using *aluf* in vv. 29–30), and (3) the chiefs of Esau by territories (vv. 40–43). The repetition suggests that the compiler had access to multiple administrative or genealogical source documents for Edom and combined them, producing the doublets and overlaps visible in the chapter.

In the final list (vv. 40–43) the chiefs are enumerated **לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לִמְקֹמֹתָם** ("by their families, by their places") — suggesting a territorial, clan-based administrative structure, not merely a personal succession list. These are chiefs of *areas* and *clans* as much as of persons.

---

**36:20–30 — The Sons of Seir the Horite: The Indigenous Population:**

The **חֹרִי** (*Hori*, "Horite") designation has generated scholarly debate. In older scholarship the Horites were often equated with the Hurrians — a people of Mesopotamian and northern Syrian origin known from Nuzi texts and other second-millennium documents. However, more recent scholarship notes that the Horites of Genesis 36 are geographically localized to the Seir/Edom region in the southern Levant, while the Hurrians were a distinct ethnic and linguistic group further north. The name *Hori* in Genesis 36 is likely a distinct Semitic term for the indigenous inhabitants of the Seir highlands — possibly meaning "cave-dwellers" (from חוֹר, *hor*, "cave, hole"). Their presence in this chapter, named and genealogized, reflects the historical reality that the Edomites did not occupy an empty territory: the Seirite highlands had a prior population that was absorbed, displaced, or intermarried with over time. Deuteronomy 2:12 and 2:22 explicitly state that Esau's descendants dispossessed the Horites of Seir, just as Israel dispossessed the Canaanites.

The Horite genealogy (vv. 20–30) names seven sons of Seir: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, Dishan. The appearance of **Zibeon** and **Anah** in both this list (as sons of Seir) and in Esau's wives' genealogy (36:2, Oholibamah's grandfather and father) confirms the intermarriage between the Edomite and Horite populations: Esau married into the Horite family of Seir, and the Horite leadership structure was absorbed into the Edomite political structure.

---

**36:24 — *ha-yemim* (הַיֵּמִם) — The Obscure Word: Hot Springs?**

Verse 24b is one of the most lexicographically contested clauses in Genesis. The text states that Anah "found the *yemim* in the wilderness while tending the donkeys of his father Zibeon." The word **הַיֵּמִם** (*ha-yemim*) is a hapax legomenon — it occurs nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible. Its meaning is therefore entirely uncertain, and the ancient translations diverge dramatically:

- **Vulgate (Jerome):** *aquas calidas* — "hot waters / hot springs." This is perhaps the best-known interpretation. Jerome (following an older tradition) understood *yemim* as related to a root meaning "warmth" and took the discovery as a geographical feature — hot springs in the desert, a notable and valuable find.
- **Samaritan Pentateuch:** reads *hayyemim* similarly as a geographical discovery — warm springs.
- **LXX (Codex Vaticanus):** *tous Iamin* — simply transliterates the word as a proper name or leaves it as *Iamin*, treating it as the name of something (or someone) Anah found. Some LXX manuscripts read *tous ōmaious* ("the mules") or variants — suggesting confusion in the Greek tradition itself.
- **Targum Onkelos:** *yimai* — understood as "giants" or "strong ones" by some rabbinic readers. Rashi follows this: Anah is said to have bred mules by crossing horses and donkeys — an act of improper hybridization — and *yemim* is taken as the term for the resulting creatures (mules). The Talmud (*Hullin* 7b) attributes the invention of mule-breeding to Anah.
- **Aquila and Theodotion:** render with a term related to "mules" or "water."

Modern lexicography remains unsettled. The options are: (1) hot springs — a geological feature; (2) mules or hybrid animals — a breeding achievement; (3) some now-unknown creature. EMET translates "hot springs" following the Jerome-Vulgate tradition as the most philologically grounded option, while noting the uncertainty. The word is preserved in transliteration in the Hebrew bracket for the reader's assessment.

The narrative parenthesis is itself unusual: within a genealogical list, the text pauses to record a specific biographical achievement of Anah — "he is the Anah who found the *yemim*." The formulation **הוּא עֲנָה** ("he is the Anah") employs the identifying pronoun *hu'* used elsewhere in the chapter to identify Esau as Edom — suggesting that this parenthesis is meant to distinguish Anah son of Zibeon from another Anah who appears in the same chapter (Anah is also listed as the father of Oholibamah in vv. 2, 14, 18, 25 — a source-critical puzzle within the chapter itself: is the Anah of v.24 the same as or different from the Anah of vv. 2 and 25?).

---

**36:31 — "Before Any King Reigned over the Sons of Israel": Editorial Anachronism:**

**וְאֵלֶּה הַמְּלָכִים אֲשֶׁר מָלְכוּ בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם לִפְנֵי מְלָךְ-מֶלֶךְ לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל** ("And these are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the sons of Israel") — this verse contains what is widely recognized as the most explicit compositional anachronism in the Pentateuch. The reference to "before any king reigned over the sons of Israel" is meaningless unless the reader already knows that Israel eventually had kings — i.e., the text is written from a perspective *after* the Israelite monarchy had begun. The monarchy of Israel begins with Saul (c. 1020 BCE). This has generated three principal explanatory positions:

1. **Mosaic authorship with prophetic foreknowledge:** Traditional interpretation (followed by Rashi, Nachmanides, and others) holds that Moses wrote this in anticipation of the Israelite monarchy promised in Deuteronomy 17:14–20, where God foresees Israel requesting a king. The statement looks *forward*, not backward, from Moses' vantage point.

2. **Post-Mosaic editorial insertion:** A minority traditional position (associated with Ibn Ezra in a famously cryptic remark in his commentary on Genesis 12:6) allows that certain passages in the Pentateuch were added by a later editor (Joshua? Samuel?), and this verse is often cited as one such passage.

3. **Documentary / source-critical position:** The verse is a marker of the document's compositional date — the king-list and its editorial introduction were compiled during or after the Israelite monarchy, as an administrative or antiquarian record placed within the larger Pentateuchal framework.

EMET translates the verse as it stands without adjudicating among these positions. The Leningrad Codex text is clear and unambiguous. The verse stands as one of the chapter's most significant textual data points for compositional-historical inquiry.

---

**36:35 — Hadad Son of Bedad: The Midian Battle:**

The notice that Hadad son of Bedad "struck Midian in the field of Moab" (*hakkeh et-Midyan bisedeh Mo'av*) is a historical reference to a conflict between Edom and Midian, geographically situated in the Moabite plateau region east of the Dead Sea. No other biblical text refers to this battle. The notice functions within the king-list formula as an identifying achievement or epithet — similar to how later king-lists in ancient Near Eastern tradition (Sumerian, Assyrian) often attach brief epithets or achievements to each ruler's name. The "field of Moab" (*sedeh Mo'av*) is a geographical designation for the Moabite tableland, the *mishor* of Moab.

---

**36:39 — Hadar vs. Hadad: The Textual Variant:**

The final king in the Edomite list is named **הֲדַר** (*Hadar*) in the Leningrad Codex Masoretic Text, while the parallel passage in **1 Chronicles 1:50–51** reads **הֲדַד** (*Hadad*). The difference is a single letter: ר (*resh*) vs. ד (*dalet*) — letters that are graphically similar in many ancient Hebrew scripts and thus susceptible to scribal confusion. The LXX Codex Vaticanus reads **Αδαδ** (*Adadd*) — supporting the Chronicles reading *Hadad* rather than the MT *Hadar*. The Syriac Peshitta also reads *Hadad*. On the basis of external manuscript evidence, the Chronicles/LXX/Peshitta *Hadad* has wider support and is likely the original reading. The Leningrad Codex's *Hadar* may represent a scribal error (resh for dalet) that entered the Masoretic tradition. EMET translates *Hadar* following the Leningrad Codex as the primary source, but records the variant.

Additionally, unlike all previous kings in the list, Hadar/Hadad receives no death notice. The list states he reigned, gives his city, wife, and wife's lineage — but the formula "and Hadar died" does not appear. This may indicate that at the time the list was compiled, Hadar/Hadad was still reigning — supporting the interpretation that the list was assembled from a contemporary administrative document, not composed retrospectively after all eight kings had died.

The name **מְהֵיטַבְאֵל** (*Mehetabel*, "God does good / who God does well by") — Hadar's queen — is one of only two women mentioned in the Edomite king-list. Her double pedigree (**בַּת-מַטְרֵד בַּת מֵי זָהָב**, "daughter of Matred, daughter of Me-zahab") is noted, unusual for a queen-consort. The name **מֵי זָהָב** (*Me-zahab*, "waters of gold") is striking and possibly a place-name.

---

**36:40–43 — The Final Chiefs List: Territorial Organization:**

The closing chiefs-list (vv. 40–43) differs structurally from the earlier ones. Where vv. 15–19 list chiefs organized by their descent from Esau's wives (Adah, Basemath, Oholibamah), vv. 40–43 list chiefs "by their families, by their places, by their names" — a territorial rather than genealogical organization. Several names appear here that do not appear in the earlier chiefs-lists (Timna, Alvah, Jetheth, Elah, Pinon, Mibzar, Magdiel, Iram) alongside names from the earlier lists (Oholibamah, Kenaz, Teman). The appearance of **Timna** as a *chief* (aluf) here is notable: Timna earlier in the chapter was Eliphaz's concubine (v.12) and Lotan's sister (v.22) — a woman's name. The *aluf Timna* in v.40 may refer to a territorial district named after the person, or may indicate a distinct individual (perhaps the district of Timna, known from Edomite copper-mining sites in the Arabah). Similarly, **Pinon** (v.41) likely corresponds to the copper-mining site of Punon/Feinan in the Wadi Arabah — an area of major metallurgical importance in the Late Bronze and Iron Ages.

The closing identification **הוּא עֵשָׂו אֲבִי אֱדוֹם** ("he is Esau, the father of Edom") formally concludes the chapter with the same equation stated at its opening: Esau *is* Edom; Edom *is* Esau. The inclusio frames the entire chapter within this identity claim.

---

**36:43 — *'arets achuzzatam* (בְּאֶרֶץ אֲחֻזָּתָם) — "The Land of Their Possession":**

The noun **אֲחֻזָּה** (*achuzzah*, "possession, holding, estate") is significant. In Genesis, this term is used for the burial ground Abraham purchases at Machpelah: **אֲחֻזַּת-קָבֶר** (*achuzzat-qaver*, "a burial-holding" — Genesis 23:4, 9, 20). The same word will describe the land allotments of Israel in Canaan throughout Numbers and Joshua. Its use here for Edom — **אֶרֶץ אֲחֻזָּתָם** ("the land of their possession/holding") — grants Edom a legitimacy of land-tenure parallel to that of Israel in Canaan. Both nations have their *achuzzah* — their God-given territorial holding. The implicit theology of Genesis 36's conclusion is that Esau's settlement of Seir is as legitimate and ordered as Jacob's settlement of Canaan: the chapter has given full legal-genealogical weight to the Edomite nation. Esau's Edom has its land, its kings, its chiefs, its histories. Jacob's Israel has its promises. Both are real.

---

**LXX Codex Vaticanus Observations (Pages 33–35):**

Codex Vaticanus pages 33–35 carry Genesis 36 in continuous Greek uncial script in three columns per page, without word-breaks. The manuscript employs the older scribal abbreviation system (nomina sacra) for certain divine names. Page 33 bears the chapter header **CAP XL** (LXX chapter enumeration — corresponding to material encompassing the end of Genesis 35 and beginning of Genesis 36 in the Greek chapter divisions). Page 34 bears the header **CAP XLI**, carrying the middle portion of Genesis 36. Page 35 bears the header **CAP XLII**, concluding Genesis 36 and opening Genesis 37. The Greek text is legible throughout all three pages, with minor ink fading in the outer margins of page 33 and some manuscript ruling visible but not intrusive. The continuous uncial script produces the same rhythmic visual pattern as the Hebrew for the genealogical and king-list sections, with the repeated Greek formulas (*kai apethanen ... kai ebasileusen anti autou*, "and X died, and Y reigned in his place") scanning clearly through multiple verses.

Key LXX variants and observations for Genesis 36:

1. **36:2 — Oholibamah's lineage:** The LXX at v.2 reads *thugatera Ana tou huiou Sebeōn tou Chorraiou* ("daughter of Ana son of Sebeōn the Horite") — reading **son** (*huiou*) where the MT has **daughter** (*bat*) for the intermediate figure. This is a significant variant: MT says Oholibamah is "daughter of Anah, daughter of Zibeon" — meaning Anah is Oholibamah's father, Zibeon is Anah's parent. The LXX reads "Anah *son* of Zibeon" — making the genealogical relationship identical to what v.24 states (Anah is son of Zibeon). The LXX's reading thus harmonizes v.2 with v.24. The MT's *bat* ("daughter") in this context may be reading the intermediate genealogical link, or it may be a scribal variant. Most modern scholars adopt the LXX reading as more coherent with the chapter's internal logic.

2. **36:11 — Zepho vs. Zephi:** The MT reads **צְפוֹ** (*Tsepho*) at v.11, while 1 Chronicles 1:36 reads **צְפִי** (*Tsephi*). The LXX reads *Sophar* — a further divergence, possibly reflecting a different Vorlage or a transliteration error. The Leningrad Codex is clear: *Tsepho*.

3. **36:12 — Timna as Eliphaz's concubine:** The LXX renders *pilegesh* (concubine) as *pallakē* (παλλακή) — the standard Greek equivalent. The LXX handles the verse without significant divergence.

4. **36:22 — Hemam vs. Homam:** MT reads **הֵימָם** (*Hemam*) as one of Lotan's sons, while 1 Chronicles 1:39 reads **הוֹמָם** (*Homam*). The LXX Vaticanus here reads *Aiman* — a transliteration that does not resolve the MT/Chronicles variant but attests a distinct tradition. The Leningrad Codex reads *Hemam* clearly.

5. **36:24 — *ha-yemim*:** The LXX at this verse reads a form that appears to render *Iamin* or a transliteration of the consonants, essentially treating the word as a proper name rather than a common noun (contrast Jerome's *aquas calidas*). This confirms that the LXX translators were as uncertain of the word's meaning as modern scholars: rather than guess, they transliterated. The Codex Vaticanus text at this point is legible and reads consistently with the LXX tradition's transliteration approach.

6. **36:26 — Dishon vs. Dishan:** The MT distinguishes between two Horite clan-names: **דִּשׁוֹן** (*Dishon*, v.21, v.25, v.26) and **דִּישָׁן** (*Dishan*, vv.21, 28, 30) — two different individuals despite the graphical similarity. The LXX tends to render both as *Daisōn* in Vaticanus, not always distinguishing them, which may be either a conflation or a reflection of a Vorlage that did not distinguish them as sharply.

7. **36:31 — "Before any king reigned over the sons of Israel":** The LXX renders *lifnei melokh-melekh livnei Yisra'el* as *pro tou basileusai basilea en Israēl* ("before a king reigned in Israel") — slightly more compact than the MT's "over the sons of Israel," but semantically equivalent. The LXX does not omit or significantly alter this editorially charged verse.

8. **36:39 — Hadar vs. Hadad:** As noted, the LXX Vaticanus reads **Αδαδ** (*Hadad*) — supporting the Chronicles/Peshitta reading rather than the Leningrad Codex's *Hadar*. The Vaticanus text is legible and clear at this point: the ד/ρ distinction maps onto the dalet/resh scribal confusion in the Hebrew.

9. **36:40–43 — The Final Chiefs List:** The LXX Vaticanus renders the final chiefs-list faithfully, though with expected transliteration variations. The name *Mibzar* is rendered *Mabsar*; *Magdiel* is rendered *Magediēl*. The closing formula *houtos Esau patēr Edom* ("this is Esau, father of Edom") corresponds exactly to the MT's *hu' Esav avi Edom*.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 36 fragments from the Dead Sea Scrolls are extant. The Leningrad Codex (primary Hebrew witness, Folios 20v–21v) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary Greek witness, Pages 33–35) are the two principal manuscript sources examined for this chapter.
